The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 120, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks on position - on level 2, clicking dots under purple blocks adds purple pixels to bottom bar; after bar fills to 9, clicking green blocks collects them without game over', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid, collectable by MOUSE after bottom bar fills', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10 with YWY dot patterns at row 11', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks - clicking adds purple pixels to bottom bar'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'purple+white at row 63, needs 9 purple pixels to unlock green block collection'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'Click all 9 dots under purple blocks to fill bottom bar (9 purple pixels), then collect all 14 green blocks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed in 50 actions - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'In progress - found solution: click 9 dots to fill bar, then collect green blocks. 3/14 collected so far.'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2 requires filling bottom bar with 9 dot clicks before green blocks can be collected without game over
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 69 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 13 actions returned the board to a state already seen 13 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 13 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 5 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Game over again after 9 green clicks (4+4+1). The interleaving didn't work - the game over still triggers after 9 green block clicks total. The bar filling doesn't help.
Let me reconsider the problem fundamentally:
1. I can only click 8-9 green blocks before game over
2. There are 14 green blocks total
3. Filling the bar doesn't prevent game over
4. Interleaving dot clicks doesn't help
New hypothesis: Maybe I need to use ACTION7 which I haven't tried at all. Or maybe the mechanic involves the player character - perhaps I need to move the player to certain positions before clicking green blocks.
Let me try ACTION7 to see what it does.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 failed with "Unknown action at index 1". This is strange since it's in valid_actions. Let me try it differently - maybe it needs to be passed differently. Let me also check the current state and try a different approach.
Wait, the error says "index 1" which is weird since I passed a list with one element at index 0. Maybe the action name is wrong or there's a different format needed.
Let me try passing it as a dict like MOUSE actions, or try a different approach entirely.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the key insight is about the ORDER of green block collection. Perhaps I need to collect them in a specific pattern - like left-to-right, top-to-bottom, or some other sequence. Or maybe I need to collect them one at a time with LEFT/RIGHT movements in between.
Let me try a completely different approach: collect green blocks one at a time, moving the player between each click.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# ACTION7 failed. Let me check current state and try a different approach.
# Maybe the key is collecting greens one at a time with player movement between

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks: {len(green)}")

# Get green positions
green_positions = []
for g in green:
    b = g['boundary']
    min_r = min(x[0] for x in b)
    min_c = min(x[1] for x in b)
    green_positions.append((min_r, min_c))
green_positions.sort()
print(f"Green positions (top-left): {green_positions}")

# New hypothesis: collect greens one at a time with LEFT/RIGHT between each
# Try: click 1 green, move LEFT, click 1 green, move RIGHT, etc.
# This tests if player movement resets some counter

# First green at (28, 10) based on previous positions
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 16},  # first green
    'LEFT'
])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Failed - unknown action error", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Clicking green blocks collects them but game over after 8-9 clicks total", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2: Game over after 8-9 green clicks. ACTION7 unavailable. Need new mechanic.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "purple+white at row 63"}, "level_log": {"level2": "All approaches failed. Trying player movement between green clicks"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect green blocks, move LEFT toward pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Test if player movement between green clicks prevents game over", "failed_probes": ["Batch green clicks: game over", "Fill bar then click green: game over", "Interleave greens with dots: game over", "ACTION7: unknown action"], "goal_model": "Collect all 14 green blocks", "recent_findings": ["ACTION7 gives unknown action error"], "world_model": "Level 2: 14 green blocks, game over after 8-9 green clicks"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
